pathophysiology_summary: Motion sickness (kinetosis) arises from sensory conflict between vestibular, visual and proprioceptive inputs (here turbulent boat motion with a static below-deck visual field), producing progressive nausea, hypersalivation, cold sweating, pallor and vomiting, worsened by head movement; a 'mal de debarquement' after-effect can persist for hours. It is benign and self-limiting. The critical ground-truth task is that acute vertigo with vomiting must have a central cause (posterior-circulation/cerebellar stroke) considered: telehealth cannot perform the discriminating examination (HINTS, gait, cerebellar signs, nystagmus characterisation), so central red flags must be screened by history (sudden severe headache, focal weakness/numbness, dysarthria, diplopia, inability to walk out of proportion) and any positive feature escalated. Here the clear kinetic trigger, habitual susceptibility and absence of central features favour benign kinetosis, but the second issue is dehydration from intractable vomiting in a patient living alone. Management is an antiemetic plus small-sip rehydration, with central and dehydration safety-nets.

differential.evidence_basis: Nausea/vertigo with cold sweats and pallor beginning on a rough boat in someone habitually motion-sensitive.
Acute vertigo + vomiting can be central — screen for headache, focal deficits, dysarthria, diplopia, gait failure; cannot be examined remotely.
Consider if symptoms persist beyond 24h without a kinetic trigger; the exact kinetic trigger favours kinetosis.
Motion-triggered vertigo preceding nausea and no diarrhoea argue against gastroenteritis.

differential_progression.scoring_note: Kinetosis leads; central vertigo (stroke) must be actively screened and dehydration assessed.

differential.evidence_basis: Clear kinetic trigger, habitual susceptibility, no central features on history — benign kinetosis; the active issue is dehydration.
Cannot be fully excluded remotely; retained as the safety-net — any central feature, or failure to improve with vestibular rest, mandates emergency assessment.

differential_progression.scoring_note: After a negative central screen, kinetosis leads for treatment BUT stroke remains a retained safety-net; manage antiemetic + rehydration with a low threshold for escalation.

differential.evidence_basis: Working diagnosis; self-limiting; antiemetic and sip rehydration; escalate if central features or failed rehydration.

differential_progression.scoring_note: Final position is benign kinetosis with central and dehydration safety-nets.

red_flags.description: Central vertigo features — sudden severe headache, focal weakness/numbness, dysarthria, diplopia, gait failure out of proportion, new deafness
red_flags.clinical_significance: Posterior-circulation/cerebellar stroke can mimic motion sickness; time-critical.

red_flags.description: Dehydration from intractable vomiting — unable to keep fluids down, reduced urine output, marked lightheadedness (living alone)
red_flags.clinical_significance: May need ED for antiemetics/IV fluids.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Attributing acute vertigo/vomiting to motion sickness without screening central (stroke) features that cannot be examined remotely.
Not addressing the inability to keep fluids down in a patient living alone.

cannot_determine_via_chat: HINTS examination, nystagmus characterisation, gait and cerebellar testing
Neurological examination for subtle focal signs
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: Antiemetic + small-sip electrolyte rehydration with vestibular rest; ED/000 for any central feature; ED if unable to rehydrate.
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: Missing a cerebellar stroke behind 'motion sickness'; under-treating dehydration.

guidelines_referenced.reference: Therapeutic Guidelines: Nausea, vomiting and vertigo
guidelines_referenced.relevant_recommendation: Motion sickness: antihistamine/anticholinergic antiemetics (e.g. promethazine, hyoscine) and vestibular rest; ondansetron for vomiting. Acute vertigo requires exclusion of central causes; escalate central red flags.
